why: why do websites ask to send notifications
how: how to send a push notification in javascript
how to ask for notification permission

1_beginner_explanation.summary: The Notifications API allows web pages to send system-level notifications that appear outside the browser window (like an email popup on your Mac or a banner on your phone). Because they can be very annoying, you MUST explicitly ask the user for permission before you can send one.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: An `alert()` is like trapping the user in a room and screaming at them until they hit 'OK'. A Notification is like sliding a polite sticky note under their door. They can read it, ignore it, or click it.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: First, JS must call `Notification.requestPermission()`, which returns a Promise resolving to 'granted', 'denied', or 'default'. If granted, instantiating `new Notification('Hello')` triggers the operating system's native notification center.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Notifications are tied to the browser. If the browser is completely closed, standard Notifications won't fire (unless combined with Service Workers and the Push API).

3_real_world_example.use_case: Alerting a user that their long file upload has finished, even if they switched to another tab.

4_common_confusion.trap: Confusing the 'Notifications API' with the 'Push API'. The Notifications API just draws the visual popup box. The Push API is what allows a server to wake up a sleeping browser and trigger that popup even when the website is closed.
4_common_confusion.fix: Notification = The UI box. Push = The delivery mechanism.

6_edge_case.quirk: Users hate notification prompts so much that modern browsers (like Chrome) will auto-block the prompt if your website asks for permission immediately on page load. You should only ask for permission after the user interacts with a button.
